title: Why Horror Is Bigger Than Ever, Plus: Gary Dauberman on ‘Annabelle Comes Home’
format: Chris Ryan joins to map out the three tiers of 2019 horror, from tentpole to iTunes deep cut, before Gary Dauberman talks through directing his first Conjuring-universe entry.

segments.heading: The State of Horror in 2019
segments.note: the tentpole, the Blumhouse tier, and the cheap stuff

films.title: Annabelle Comes Home
films.note: The seventh or eighth Conjuring-universe film, and the reason for this episode; Dauberman's directing debut.

films.title: Us
films.note: Jordan Peele's follow-up to Get Out; both hosts saw it at South by Southwest with Universal's chairman in the room.

films.title: A Quiet Place
films.note: John Krasinski's directorial debut and one of the biggest horror hits ever; last year's version of this conversation.

films.title: Escape Room
films.note: The low-key big hit of the year, per Sean.

films.title: Pet Sematary
films.note: A Paramount movie that aspired to be glossier than its budget allowed.

films.title: Brightburn
films.note: Barely seen, but Sean liked its use of the superhero premise.

films.title: The Dead Don't Die
films.note: A small Focus release with a stacked cast; Jim Jarmusch's zombie movie.

films.title: Doctor Sleep
films.note: Mike Flanagan's Shining sequel, due later in the year.

films.title: It
films.note: The unlikely box-office giant that helped set off this whole cycle.

segments.heading: Chris Ryan's iTunes Deep Cuts
segments.note: how to find good horror on VOD without spoiling it for yourself

films.title: Starfish
films.note: A meditative, Terrence Malick-y post-apocalypse mystery Chris found trawling iTunes.

films.title: Head Count
films.note: A Candyman-style campfire-ghost-story movie; the one Chris actually recommends without hedging.

segments.heading: Growing Up on Horror: Shocker and Wes Craven

films.title: Shocker
films.note: Chris's touchstone for how disreputable horror felt as a teenager in Wes Craven's post-Nightmare years.

films.title: The Serpent and the Rainbow
films.note: Craven's voodoo movie, better than its reputation suggested at the time.

segments.heading: How Much Should a Trailer Give Away?
segments.note: the case for going in blind

films.title: Midsommar
films.note: Next week's episode subject; Ari Aster's follow-up to Hereditary, which Sean and Chris saw together.

films.title: Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark
films.note: Guillermo del Toro produces this anthology adaptation of the illustrated short-story collection.

films.title: Ma
films.note: Sean's example of a movie whose trailer told him everything before he'd seen it.

films.title: Crawl
films.note: Alexandre Aja's alligator-in-the-house movie, starring Chris Ryan favorite Barry Pepper and Kaya Scodelario.

films.title: Zombieland: Double Tap
films.note: A sequel nobody needed, with the whole original cast back anyway.

films.title: The Lighthouse
films.note: Robert Eggers' follow-up to The Witch; Pattinson and Dafoe, shot in high-contrast black and white.

films.title: Hereditary
films.note: Sean's example of elevated horror working best when you don't go in expecting a possession movie.

segments.heading: It Chapter Two and the State of the Conjuring Universe

films.title: The Nun
films.note: Not their favorite of the Conjuring spinoffs, but still a hit.

films.title: Annabelle Comes Home
films.note: Dauberman's directing debut, built around the Warrens bringing the doll home to the artifact room, all in one house over one night.

films.title: Annabelle: Creation
films.note: Where Dauberman first dug into the doll's backstory.

films.title: The Conjuring
films.note: Home to the matchstick scene Dauberman calls one of his favorites in horror, and the effect he chased in Annabelle Comes Home.

films.title: Toy Story 4
films.note: Dauberman's answer to the show's closing question about the last great thing he's seen.

Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark.title: Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark
